data.content_ref: inline:The mechanism's derivation is algebraically incoherent. It states the net-benefit condition as R·0.29 > (1−P)·0.71, then claims this simplifies to 'if R > 1−e and P > e, the critic's positive likelihood ratio exceeds the class prior ratio, guaranteeing net benefit.' But R > 1−e and P > e are conditions on the critic's raw performance, not on the likelihood ratio; the positive likelihood ratio is P/(1−R), and the condition R·0.29 > (1−P)·0.71 does not algebraically reduce to R > 1−e ∧ P > e. For k=7, e≈2/7≈0.286, so R > 0.714 and P > 0.286 — yet plugging P=0.29, R=0.72 into the actual inequality gives 0.72×0.29 = 0.209 vs (1−0.29)×0.71 = 0.504, which VIOLATES the inequality. The claimed sufficient condition is therefore false: it does not guarantee net benefit even when satisfied.
